title: I Studied the Top 100 Stocks... The Results Shocked Me (VCP Pattern)
transcript: In today's video, we showcase an interesting study I've done on the top 100 performers of the last 12 months. I checked the charts of these winners for the volatility contraction patterns, VCP, and the data amazed us. The idea behind this study is simple. Would you have enough trading opportunities if you focused on only one setup to trade? That's an important question to answer because for most struggling traders, the information overload is frustrating. Too many successful traders, too many styles, indicators, and patterns. To top it all, the emotional stress of trading with real money makes it even more overwhelming. That's why it's important to step back and go slow. It's better to learn and grow slowly than to fail quickly and leave. As a beginner, if you were just trading clean VCP breakouts with no other setup, no other strategy, how would you have fared? Let's find out in this study. The advantage of trading VCPs is that they take their own time to develop and therefore help you avoid rushed trades. You can take your time to follow stocks and observe price movements before pressing the trade button. To trade VCPs, we first lay down the basic scanning ground rule. We look for stocks that are up the most from their 52-week lows. We add some more filters like the market cap being over $100 million, dollars price above $1, and dollar volume above $10 million dollars to weed out illiquid microcaps and further shorten the scanned list. If you run this list in a raging bull market, you will get several hundred names to screen. If that's the case, we pick the top 10% of the performers to look for VCPs. However, if you're scanning at the start of a bull run, you won't get that many names, which will make your job easier. That's also the best time to be trading VCPs as many stocks emerging strong from a bear market or correction tends to lead the market and deliver multi-bagger gains. On the other hand, in a bear market or severe correction, this list will be even shorter with no good setups to trade. Even if you find some setups, you should avoid trading them because the best of the setups fail frequently in bear markets. Next, we set the ground rules for clean VCPs. We look for VCPs with at least three contractions, narrow ranges within each contraction, and clear tightness on the right side of the base before the breakout. Our focus is on clean, obvious setups that are easy to identify. By maintaining strict criteria for setup quality, we avoid forcing trades in stocks with choppy and erratic price action. Next, the last contraction should not be too deep. Prefer setups with a last contraction depth lower than 2x the average daily range or ADR of the stock. So, if the ADR is 5%, the last contraction must not be deeper than 10%. This allows for manageable and logical stop losses when stops are placed at the low of the last contraction. Next, the entry is assumed to have happened not more than 5% from the breakout level, which is the high of the last contraction. When a breakout happens with a gap up, you could enter on the break of opening range highs on 1-min, 5-minutes, or the 1-hour candle. Also, the entry must allow for a stop loss of less than 10% when the stop is placed at the low of the last contraction. If the low of the last contraction is more than 10%, we can place the stop below the low of the breakout day if it allows for a stop loss of less than 10%. When entering on opening range highs, the stop will be placed at the low of the day. Next, we prescribe the exit rules. We use the 10-day and 20-day exponential moving averages or EMAs as trailing stops to manage and exit winning positions. To eliminate subjectivity from the process, we apply a rules-based approach based on the stock's average daily range or ADR. For fast-moving stocks with an ADR of 10% or higher, we use the 10-day EMA as the trailing stop, allowing us to lock in gains in quick movers. For slower-moving stocks with an ADR below 10%, we use the 20-day EMA, giving the position more room to fluctuate without being prematurely stopped out by normal price movements. With the basic rules now defined, let's get to the results of the study. The top 100 performers of the last year had 36 clean, tradeable VCPs. 33% of these occurred in August and September 2025 in raging bull markets. 22% happened in January when the market stabilized post the November fall, and 17% occurred in April 2026 when the market recovered from the February to March dip. Expectedly, the correction months of November, December, February, and March together accounted for 11% of VCP setups. The data confirms that the absence of setups in correction is a natural savior for patient swing traders because no setups means no trade. The average risk across all trades was 6%, while the average return was 69%. That's over 11x risk-to-reward ratio. Some of the best performers were SNDK, IREN, ALMS, DELL, MXL, and AIP. As is always the case, most of these stocks were from the l

title: Profitable Trading Strategy: Master the Volatility Contraction Pattern (VCP)
transcript: hi all in today's video we discuss one of the most popular setups in swing trading the volatility contraction pattern or the vcp the pattern was championed by the legendary Trader Mark minini in his first book trade like a stock market wizard which we've covered in detail in this video Mark emphasized that the pattern has been at the Forefront of his trading strategy and this helped him win the US investing Championship once in 1997 and again in 2021 when he broke all records in the $1 million Capital category with a 343 gain in a single year but how did this pattern rise to prominence and why is it so powerful in identifying high potential stocks let's dive in before we delve into the details of the pattern I want to make it clear the vcp is most useful to catch the continuation of a move in a trending instrument therefore an existing trend is the prerequisite for the vcp to be effective Mark minini identifies Trends using his Trends template that emphasizes the position of the stock relative to three key moving averages the 50-day 150-day and 200 day the stock price is distance from the 52- we low and high and its relative strength as published in the Investor's Business Daily here is the full template feel free to pause the video to get a full grasp of the rules the basic idea is to find a trending price and join the next move up the vcp isn't just about patterns it's about psychology it reflects the behavior of institutional investors accumulating shares over time leading to a breakout when demand overtakes Supply it works in all trading instruments including stocks Commodities currencies and cryptocurrencies the pattern is character ized by a series of price consolidations where each successive pullback becomes smaller this tightening of price action reflects reduced volatility signaling that selling pressure is diminishing there are three stages in the pattern the first is wide price wings which is the initial volatility as the stock forms a base the wild swing is when the price is most violent it generally occurs from the in pressure of Traders and investors who made money in the earlier move as the price drops and the mark to Market losses start to show the Traders are gripped by uncertainty and fear driving larger price swings at some point the wild swings are supported by institutional buying marking the bottom of the wild swing stage then comes the smaller pullback stage in which each contraction is less severe than the previous one each contraction represents diminishing selling intensity as sellers exhaust themselves while buyers grow more aggressive the number of contractions that occur in the price will generally vary from stock to stock if the demand for the stock is high the contractions will be less severe and fewer however when the demand is low or when the institutional investors are still figuring out the worthiness of the stock the contractions will be severe and more frequent the last stage of the vcp is the breakout point in the best of the patterns the late contractions are small forming a very tight area when the price moves in a narrow range the volumes at these points dry down as there is limited supply of stocks near the high as the demand outstrips Supply at this juncture the price breaks out with high volumes the best time to take an entry in the setup was when the price forms a tight area not too far from the previous highs preferably in the 0 to 10% range the vcp can come in multiple shapes and forms for example a triangle in which the highs and lows of the contractions are joined through trend lines forming the triangle a range or flat base in which the trend lines run parallel when drawn on the highs and lows this is something I look for in particular when trading breakouts cup with handle in which the price pattern resembles a cup with a handle with the handle being the tight area before the breakout and the last is the flag a powerful pattern that resembles a small flag when the trend lines are drawn connecting the highs and lows of the pattern let's see the entry and exit rules in the pattern most of the time the entry happens when the price breaks above the last contraction and touches new highs with volumes the stop loss is placed at the low of that contraction creating a great low risk High reward opportunity these patterns work best in trending markets when you can clock High success rates having said that if you can maintain low risk and high reward even a low success rate can be profitable you can see here that my win rate is nothing spectacular but with a solid risk reward ratio I still make solid Returns the setups do work in sideways markets too but not as well as they do in trending markets following an upward Trend certainly improves the odds of success some Traders advise taking profits when you achieve the desired reward relative to the risk of the trade so for example if you bought a stock at a breakout of $100 with a $5 stop loss and you're aiming for a r

However, after the wash out occurs, there seems to be a bottom and then it's almost as if the buyer demand picks back up. You might consider perhaps the weaker hands left here. Once again, the inverted cup and handle also has a handle. A handl

title: Cup With Handle Pattern - Common Mistake
transcript: हेलो फ्रेंड्स, वेलकम बैक। आई एम विवेक सिंघल फ्रॉम ट्रेडिंग विवेक.com और जैसे मेरे पास WhatsApp पर काफी सारे फीडबैक्स आते हैं या क्वेरीज आती हैं। तो जो अपने सब्सक्राइबर्स हैं, जो फॉलोअर्स हैं बेसिकली जो स्टूडेंट्स हैं या फिर हम सब लोग हैं। हम कुछ भी नाम दें। अह तो वह क्या प्रॉब्लम फेस कर रहे हैं इस बात का पता चलता है। तो एक जो कॉमन मिस्टेक काफी लोग करते हैं वो कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न को फॉलो करने में करते हैं। उसको एक प्रॉपर चार्ट पैटर्न बनाने में करते हैं। और वो कॉमन मिस्टेक क्या है? मैं आपको एक पर्टिकुलर एग्जांपल लेकर बताता हूं। जैसे और हम फॉर एग्जांपल यहां पर लेते हैं कैंप्स का एग्जांपल लेते हैं। और कैम्स में मैं एक बार इस मूविंग एवरेज को भी हटा देता हूं एक बार और उसके बाद जैसे हम यहां पर कई बार जो मिस्टेक करते हैं वो इस तरीके की मिस्टेक करते हैं कि विवेक भाई इज इट ए करेक्ट कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न? कि हमने यहां पर इस कप को यहां से इस तरीके से ड्रॉ किया और ये हमारा हैंडल बन गया। तो क्या यह करेक्ट कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न है? तो इसमें जो सबसे बड़ी मिस्टेक है वह यह कि जब भी कप की बिगिनिंग हो वो कभी भी मिड ऑफ द फॉल से नहीं हो सकती। कप की बिगिनिंग टॉप से हो सकती है। जैसे कप की बिगिनिंग या तो यहां से हो सकती थी जो इसका एक प्रॉपर टॉप है। और टॉप कैसे बनेगा? टॉप नीचे से ऊपर जाने पर बनेगा। ऊपर से लगातार नीचे आ रहा है तो नहीं बनेगा। जैसे यहां से नीचे से ऊपर गया तो ये टॉप बन सकता था। लेकिन हम मिड ऑफ द फॉल को हम टॉप नहीं कह सकते। या फिर हमारा टॉप यहां पर बन सकता था। इस लेवल पर या फिर हमारा इस लेवल पर बन सकता था। लेकिन मिड ऑफ द फॉल इज नॉट द करेक्ट स्टार्टिंग ऑफ कप विथ हैंडल पैटर्न। हम जैसे एक और एग्जांपल यहां पर लेते हैं। nstle का एग्जांपल मैं आपको लेता हूं। अह nसle इंडिया और यहां पर अगर हम देखें और हम एक यहां पर लाइन ड्रॉ करने लगे हैं इस तरीके से कि क्या ये सही मल्टी हैंडल वाला कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न है कि हमने एक यहां पर ये कप बनाया फिर ये एक हैंडल बनाया फिर ये एक और हैंडल बना फिर ये एक और हैंडल बना नो दिस इज़ नॉट ए करेक्ट वन यहां से कप की बिगिनिंग नहीं हो सकती कप की बिगिनिंग हमेशा हमेशा ही जो एक टॉप होगा टेंपरेरी टॉप होगा जैसे जैसे ये एक टेंपरेरी डॉप है। यहां से अगर इस तरह की कोई भी हमारा कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न बनता तो वो वैलिड होता या कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न हमारा यहां से बनता तो वो वैलिड होता। ये यहां पर वैलिड नहीं है। इसी तरीके से अगर मैं एक और एग्जांपल यहां पर एनmडीced का एनmडीced में अगर हम देखें तो जैसे ये टॉप बना तो क्या यहां से आगे कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न बनाया जा सकता है? नहीं। इससे लेकिन क्या इसको कफ विद हैंडल पैटर्न माना जा सकता है कि यहां से थोड़ा सा ऊपर चला गया वापस से और उसके बाद में क्या हम इसको कफ विथ हैंडल पैटर्न मान सकते हैं क्या हम इसको टॉप मान सकते हैं ये एक बहुत ही सब्जेक्टिव है कि इसको कप विथ हैंडल पैटर्न माना जाए या नहीं माना जाए तो जहां पर भी इस तरह के डाउट्स हैं मैं तो मैं हमेशा ये कहता हूं डाउट्स में हमें कभी काम नहीं करना चाहिए डाउट्स में छोड़ देना चाहिए तो इससे पहले जो दो एग्जांपल्स थे जैसे nstle का था वो क्लेरिटी के साथ था तो जहां पर क्लियर है वहां तो कोई डाउट ही नहीं है। जैसे हमने कैंप्स में लिया था कैंप्स में भी जहां क्लियर है कोई डाउट नहीं है कि हमें काम करना चाहिए या नहीं करना चाहिए नहीं करना चाहिए। जहां कोई पैटर्न नहीं है वहां काम नहीं करना चाहिए। लेकिन जैसे हमने ये पर्टिकुलर एग्जांपल लिया एनएमडीसी का। एनएमडीसी के केस में जैसे यहां डाउटफुल है तो डाउटफुल के केस में हमें छोड़ देना चाहिए। मेरा कहना यह है और या फिर आप इसको वापस से बैक टेस्ट करके देखो कि आपका रिजल्ट क्या निकल के आता है। एक और एग्जांपल जैसे हम यहां पर pidlite indstries का लेते हैं। pidlite में हम यहां पर हमने चार्ट ओपन किया। अब अगर आप देखोगे तो क्या हम इसको कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न मान सकते हैं। नो इसको कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न यहां से तो बिगनिंग पे नहीं मान सकते। क्योंकि हमारा जो कप है वो एक टॉप से स्टार्ट होना चाहिए। वो मिड ऑफ द फॉल से स्टार्ट नहीं होना चाहिए। ये बड़ा सिंपल सा पॉइंट है। तो ये तो हो गई कॉमन मिस्टेक। अब किस तरह से चीजों को हम ले सकते हैं? जैसे इसी पर्टिकुलर केस में पिडलाइट के ही केस में हम इसको तो कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न नहीं मान सकते क्योंकि कप कैन नॉट स्टार्ट फ्रॉम मिड ऑफ़ द फॉल। सो इट कैन नॉट बी कंसीडर्ड एज अ कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न। लेकिन इसी में अगर आप देखोगे तो इस लाइन को आप कप से ड्रॉ करने के बदले थोड़ा सा आगे से यहां से ड्रॉ करो तो आपको एक अलग से पैटर्न नजर आ जाएगा। और ये हमारा रिवर्स हेड एंड शोल्डर पैटर्न है। तो यहां पर एक ये शोल्डर हो गया। ये बेसिकली हेड बना था। लेकिन राइट शोल्डर क्योंकि हेड से नीचे चला गया। तो ये भी एक शोल्डर बन गया। और ये अब हेड बन गया। हेड के बाद फिर एक ये शोल्डर बना। फिर एक ये शोल्डर बना और ये प्रॉपर मल्टी जिसको हम कॉम्प्लेक्स हेड एंड शोल्डर पैटर्न कहते हैं। तो ये कॉम्प्लेक्स रिवर्स हेड एंड शोल्डर पैटर्न है। ये एक पैटर्न है। तो बहुत बार आपको क्या लगेगा? आपको क्या देखने में आएगा कि जहां एक बड़ा सा कप विद हैंडल पैटर्न आप समझ रहे थे लेकिन वो मिड ऑफ द फॉल से था तो हमें उस मिड ऑफ़ द फॉल से तो छोड़ देना चाहिए लेकिन उसके आगे आपको छोटा सा स्मॉलर साइज का रिवर्स हेड एंड शोल्डर पैटर्न या कफ विद हैंडल पैटर्न दिख सकता है। अब यहां पर जो साइज है साइज से कोई इंपैक्ट नहीं है। चाहे ये जितना मर्जी छोटा शेप हो, चाहे जितना मर्जी कम टाइम होराइजन में हो या लगर टाइम होराइजन में हो या बिग शेप हो, कोई फर्क नहीं पड़ता। अगर ये प्रॉपर बना है, वैलिड बना है तो ये हमेशा वैलिड है। अब जैसे मैं एक और एग्जांपल यहां पर लेता हूं। एनएसआईएल एनएसआईएल को अगर हम देख

title: The Pattern Behind the Market's Biggest Winners
transcript: Hi all. Similar to the previous video, we'll examine an interesting study on the biggest stock market winners of the past year. But this time, I analyzed every chart on the winners list to identify cup with handle patterns and answer a simple question. If a trader ignored every other setup and focused exclusively on the cup with handle pattern, would there still be enough trading opportunities? The prior study looked at the volatility contraction pattern or VCP, the results of which you can find in this video using the links below. Now, identifying setups before the stocks took off needs a lot of practice. Kalamagous says that he went through thousands of stock charts to practice identifying great setups. The traders who spend that kind of time to understand markets ultimately excel in trading. I have also spent three decades trading markets and practiced one particular setup called the lateral consolidation pattern. It's similar to the Darvas box pattern, a pattern that helps Nicolas Darvas, a dancer turned trader, to transform $36,000 into $2.25 million. Despite my approach being rather passive, the setup has helped me achieve some nice returns with controlled risk. Drawing on my deep understanding of the setup, I built a bespoke scanner designed to identify these lateral consolidation patterns automatically. It has reduced my weekend scanning time from hours to just a few minutes, allowing me to focus solely on executing trades. I believe this scanner, combined with my strategy, can help busy individuals find positive expectancy in the markets with just a few minutes of work each weekend. To learn more about the scanner, community, the trading dashboard, and to download my free strategy, check the links in the description below. Coming back to the CWH pattern, we first lay down the basic scanning ground rule. We look for stocks that are up the most from their 52-week lows. We add some more filters like the market cap over $100 million, price over $1, and dollar volume above $10 million to weed out illiquid microcaps and further shorten the scanned list. If there are too many names in the list as a result of a strong market, we pick the top 10% of the performers to look for cup handle patterns. Although, when scanning at the start of a bull run, there might not be too many names. That is also the best time to be trading the pattern as many stocks emerging strongly from a bear market or correction tends to lead the market and deliver multi-bagger gains. On the other hand, in a bear market or severe correction, this list will be even shorter with few good setups to trade. Even if you find some setups, you should avoid trading them or trade small because the best of the setups can fail frequently in bear markets. Next, we lay down the ground rules for identifying and trading the pattern. We're looking for clean cup with handle patterns on the daily chart. A clear, deep cup followed by a shallow handle. Clean setups are easy to spot. If you have to force yourself to see the pattern, it's probably not a high-quality setup. We are looking for a minimum of 5 weeks of consolidation with the breakout occurring in the fifth week or later. Although William O'Neil originally specified a minimum pattern length of 7 weeks, we relax that requirement because today's markets move much faster than they did in his era. The shakeout of weak holders and takeover by institutions happens much faster in today's age. Therefore, traders must adapt to this new reality and be willing to trade shorter duration cup with handle patterns. Next, the entry happens no more than 5% from the breakout level, which is the high of the handle. The setup should give us a logical stop loss, typically 10% or less. In case the low of the handle, which is the standard stop loss in these trades, is deeper than 10%, we put the stop loss at the low of the breakout candle. When the breakout happens through gap ups, we enter at the opening range highs using the 1-minute, 5-minutes, or 1-hour candles, placing the stop at the low of the day. Next, we prescribe the exit rules. We use the 10-day and 20-day exponential moving averages or EMAs as trailing stops to manage and exit winning positions. To eliminate subjectivity from the process, we apply a rules-based approach based on the stock's average daily range or ATR. For fast-moving stocks with an ATR of 10% or higher, we use the 10-day EMA as the trailing stop, allowing us to lock in gains in quick movers. For slower-moving stocks with an ATR below 10%, we use the 20-day EMA, giving the position more room to fluctuate without being prematurely stopped out by normal price movements. As I mentioned in the last video, this study suffers from survivorship bias because it analyzes only the biggest winners in hindsight. However, the objective is simply to determine whether focusing exclusively on the cup with handle pattern would provide enough opportunities to capture moves in these winning stocks. Even if you

title: Chart Patterns: Cup With Handle
transcript: I'm Matthew Galgani with part two in our series on how to use chart patterns to get in early on winning stocks in this video we'll talk about the cup with handle whether it's Walmart in 1980 Apple in 2004 or green mountain coffee roasters in 2009 year after year many of the best stocks form this pattern just before they make a major price move the name comes from the fact that it looks like a teacup viewed from the side here's the cup here's the handle let's go over the basic elements to look for then see the cup with handle pattern in action first look for a prior uptrend of at least 30-percent after the stock has made that climb it pulls back to digest those gains here's the back story as the stock is making this nice gain it becomes obvious to most investors people who bought earlier are sitting on some nice profits but many folks mainly those who don't use charts to time their buys will jump on the bandwagon not realizing they're buying too late what happens next savvy investors who bought at the beginning of the uptrend start cashing in some profits and it's likely the market uptrend that helped propel the stocks earlier move is now slipping into a downtrend all of that puts selling pressure on the stock and it starts forming the left side of that cup if the stock has the big earnings growth and other can slim traits you look for fund managers and other institutional investors will step in to support the stock at some point the bottom of the cup starts to form when these big investors stop selling aggressively and start buying the depth of the cup should be around 15 to 30 percent in a severe bear market the decline might be 40 percent or more once a bottom has been established the stock starts climbing higher to form the right side of the base that's a sign institutional investors are picking up shares then the handle starts to form it should form in the upper half of the cup and the decline within the handle is typically around 10 to 12 percent the handle is basically a shake out of weaker hold who are those weaker holders the folks who bought too late in the last uptrend they took a nasty hit when the stock made this big decline now they're no longer worried about making a profit they just want a smaller loss so when the share price gets closer to the price they paid for it back here they sell that's what causes the handle to form but here's the key the volume in the handle should be light that tells you it's just these weaker holders that are selling the more committed investors the fund managers and other big players who have been buying up shares as the stocks form the right side of the cup those folks are sitting tight because they expect more profits from the stock the total length of a cup with handle including the handle area should be at least seven weeks and note that that peak in the handle is the stock's most recent area of resistance it bumped its head there and came back down so that's our new testing ground if the stock can push through that on heavy volume that's a sign of strength and could mean the stock is poised for a big run so the ideal buy point for a cup with handle is calculated by adding 10 cents to the peak and the handle we add 10 cents just to make sure the stock is truly breaking through that area of resistance and not just bumping its head against it once again the key is volume as the stock breaks through the ideal buy point you want volume to be at least 40 to 50 percent higher than normal that tells you institutional investors are heavily buying the stock you want to buy as close to the ideal buy point as possible and you can set trade triggers with your broker ahead of time to help make sure you catch those breakouts the buying range is from the ideal buy point up to 5% above that price avoid buying a stock once it's climbed beyond that 5% range buying late makes it more likely you'll take a loss and be forced to sell if the stock pulls back a little bit after the breakout as stocks often do here's another important point remember how we said the general market was probably falling into a downtrend at the same time the left side of the cup was starting to form the direction of the overall market plays a big role on the breakout - once the market downtrend that started over here ends and a new Uptrend begins that's usually when you see breakouts occur case in point Chipotle Mexican Grill Chipotle made a nice run in the first half of 2010 that served as the prior uptrend of at least 30% then in late June the overall market began to weaken the outlook in the big picture column changed from confirmed uptrend to uptrend under pressure and finally to market in correction around the same time Chipotle pulled back to form this cup with handle look how it found support at the bottom of that cup after dipping lower during the week it rebounded to close at the top of its range and above the benchmark 10 week moving average line and look at the volume it was 115 percent higher

title: ADVANCED Cup and Handle Chart Pattern Trading Strategy (For Pros Only)
transcript: Hey, what's up guys? This is ZQu here. So, in this video, I'm going to show you one of the most effective trading patterns known as the advanced cup and handle pattern. So, you know, many traders, they use this pattern the wrong way, and that results in a lot of losing trades. And I'm going to show you how to fix that. So, the advanced cup and handle pattern is like a goal mine when you learn how to use it right. And I'm going to show you how to spot it the right way, how to avoid traps, and how to make the most money out from it using this special combination that I have created. You're going to like this one. Let's go. [Music] [Applause] [Music] So first off, what is the cup and handle pattern? So the cup and handle pattern is a chart formation that indicates a possible upward trend right in any vehicle. So whether you are trading stocks, forex, commodities or crypto, it works the same. So it gets the name from its like a teacup shape, right? Which is made out of two key segments. So the pattern is in an uptrend, whether it's an uptrend or downtrend, right? It first forms a U shape or what we call as the cup, which is followed by a consolidation, which is what we call a handle. Here's how it looks like. So now let's break this down the cup. So what happens is the price rise and then pulls back to form this rounded bottom and then after that it climbs up again. So this creates what? The cup shape which is marked by what? An increasing highs and lows. So this signals what a upwards momentum. So then the next one that forms the shape the handle. So once the cup is completed and and price reaches the top there is a minor pullback or a consolidation which is what we call the handle before price tries to break above the resistance level and that is the neck line. So here's the thing most traders they rush to buy when price breaks above the resistance level assuming that it's going to keep climbing. But hey we know better right? The cup and handle isn't just about breakout. What is important is that this setup is about building momentum. So when you combine this with the right confirmation signal, it's going to give you a high probability trade setup and and it's not just like ah you enter a trade and hope that it will work. This is going to be a high probability trading setup. Now here's the thing. This cup of handle pattern can be either continuation from a previous uptrend or it can be from a previous downtrend and is strong enough to form a reversal to turn the downtrend into an uptrend. So this is this pattern is strong and and you want to add this to your trading library, your trade setups. Now depending on the time frame that you're looking at. So obviously if you can see this right it takes time to form an entire cup and handle and it takes quite a number of bars right quite a number of bars and that's why that's why it is so powerful because the bigger the setup the more powerful the move is going to be when it goes. Now that you know the cup and handle let's now talk about the neck line. So this is a horizontal resistance line that is drawn at the top of the cup where price tend to store before making the break out. Next breakout point. This is a buy signal that occurs when price breaks above the neckline. So now once price broke from the breakout point which is the neckline, right? We're going to add something here as an extra confirmation to increase the probability of it working out. All right, you ready? It's the volume. So, the volume tells us the strength of the market at that current moment. So, it is important to gauge the strength of the breakout. So, here's what to look for. The volume should decrease during the formation of the cup and then increase as price breaks above the handle. So this indicates strong buying interest at that moment. The formation of the cup isn't going to be too strong because it's gathering momentum but it should increase when price breaks above the handle. So now next breakout confirmation. title: I made a Market Simulation to see if Patterns are Real
transcript: Have you ever wanted an answer to a problem, but no one can agree on anything? All right, here's the problem. There are fake gurus out there who will sell you on secrets to making money that don't actually work. Worse yet, these gurus are proven liars who make their money from lying to beginner traders who don't know anything. No. On the other hand, there are YouTubers who call out fake gurus, claiming that patterns aren't real, and it's just a weird psychology thing with how people buy and sell. And it's the male equivalent of astrology. So, the question still stands, is it real or not? Well, if we really want to put this to the test, we'll have to do it ourselves and build an entire market simulation from scratch. In a market, there are people who want to sell a product and people who want to buy it. If we throw a whole bunch of these people into a simulation, it'll create a little microcosm market. But right away, we run into a problem. How do these people make up prices? One person could make up $100 and another say $2. There's no rhyme or reason to this. To fix this, we'll use one piece of human psychology and then the rest of the video, I promise, will be math focused. We're trying to make this as unbiased as possible, but we need this detail here. So, if the price is $100, the new prices that traders pick will be up or down 1%. At a random value. So, if someone heard hot dogs are going for $5 a piece, it's pretty unlikely they'll suggest $50 as the next offer. So, our code makes a price like $4.95 the likely next offer. All right, with a thousand of these traders in our simulation, it just kind of sits there. So, there's something called a maker, and they make the order in the order book, and then a taker who takes the order at whatever price is there. And if everyone is happy with the current prices, nothing's going to change. They just keep placing the same orders over and over again. title: 3 Must-Know Algorithms for Automating Chart Pattern Trading in Python
transcript: in this video we're going to look at three essential algorithms for automating chart patterns and other forms of technical analysis such as support and resistance levels the three algorithms provide different approaches for systematically identifying local tops and bottoms in the price many chart patterns are built from local tops and bottoms for example The Head and Shoulders pattern is built around five local turning points in price many other chart patterns are also built from local tops and bottoms for a human identifying these turning points is quite easy you just look but for a computer it's not so straightforward I'll explain three different algorithms to identify turning points and show python implementations after that I'll compare and contrast each algorithm in the future I'll create videos that make use of these algorithms to identify well-known chart patterns bind horizontal support and resistance levels and to data mine novel chart patterns so subscribe to not miss those our first algorithm is the rolling window method it identifies local tops and bottoms by checking if a point is the highest or lowest point compared to its neighboring points for example let's look at this point here marked with red we verify that it is a local top by seeing if it is greater than its two adjacent points marked with white in this case we only shift one point on either side of the point in question the number of adjacent points to check is the one parameter for this algorithm I will call this parameter the order this red point is a local top of order one but if we check the two adjacent points we find a value greater than it marked with orange thus this point is not a top of order two here is every local top of order 1 in this sample here's every top with order two and here's every top with order three as the order increases the number of local tops we find decreases as we're being more selective local bottoms can be found in the same way but instead we check if the point in question is less than its neighboring points instead here's every local bottom of order one order two and order three it's important to note since we're focusing on a trading application these local tops and bottoms are identified with a lag equal to the order parameter for example the top of order 2 marked here in red is not confirmed until the time marked in green let's look at the code this function checks if there is a the top of a given order confirmed that the current index the index of the potential top is delayed by the order given to avoid looking into the future we check if there is a point that is greater than it on either side if there is not we have found a top bottoms are found in the same way but with a less than comparison instead all local extremes can be found by looping through each candle in the data and checking if there is a high or a low if either is found we record the index of confirmation the index of the extreme and the price of the extreme the same algorithm is implemented in the scipy library called ARG Rel extrema but if you choose to use it be careful you are not cheating with future data while this algorithm can be used with high and low prices of candles I think using just the clothes is better because if the market made information like this a local high and low of order 2 would be identified on the same candle which seems illogical to me I've seen this algorithm called many different things and it is a very obvious way to find local tops and bottoms or second algorithm is directional change sometimes called zigzag this algorithm identifies tops and bottoms when the price has retraced a given amount from its most recent high or low price this algorithm always produces alternating local tops and bottoms let's slow down this visualization to better explain at this point in time the last confirmed local extreme is a bottom meaning the next local extreme will be a top this orange candle is the highest high we have seen since our confirmed bottom the yellow line is our confirmation line it is three percent below the high of the orange candle the highest high once the price closes below this level the price is retraced Enough from the high and the top is confirmed at the high of the orange candle like so now that we've confirmed to this top we have a new orange candle it is the lowest low since our confirmed top this blue line is our new confirmation line it is three percent above the low of the orange candle once the price closes above the Blue Line the bottom is confirmed and the process repeats the percentage we use to define a retracement is the parameter for this algorithm here we use three percent if it is set to a higher value local tops and bottoms will be found less often set to a lower value and they will be found more often the amount of lag to confirm tops and bottoms is undefined it varies each time let's look at the code we have a function directional change that takes in the close high and low and si
